title: Content-Driven Local Response: Supporting Sentence-Level and Message-Level Mobile Email Replies With and Without AI
authors: Tim Zindulkatim.zindulka@uni-bayreuth.de0009-0009-1972-351XUniversity of BayreuthBayreuthGermany,Sven Gollersven.goller@uni-bayreuth.de0000-0001-5263-5372University of BayreuthBayreuthGermany,Florian Lehmannflorian.lehmann@uni-bayreuth.de0000-0003-0201-867XUniversity of BayreuthBayreuthGermanyandDaniel Buschekdaniel.buschek@uni-bayreuth.de0000-0002-0013-715XUniversity of BayreuthBayreuthGermany
abstract: Mobile emailing demands efficiency in diverse situations, which motivates the use of AI. However, generated text does not always reflect how people want to respond. This challenges users with AI involvement tradeoffs not yet considered in email UIs. We address this with a new UI concept called Content-Driven Local Response (CDLR), inspired by microtasking. This allows users to insert responses into the email by selecting sentences, which additionally serves to guide AI suggestions. The concept supports combining AI for local suggestions and message-level improvements. Our user study (N=126) compared CDLR with manual typing and full reply generation. We found that CDLR supports flexible workflows with varying degrees of AI involvement, while retaining the benefits of reduced typing and errors. This work contributes a new approach to integrating AI capabilities: By redesigning the UI for workflows with and without AI, we can empower users to dynamically adjust AI involvement.

paragraphs: Emails challenge us to stay responsive and responsible – anytime, anywhere. With mobile devices, people wish to stay responsive despite contextual challenges: They might lack the time and attention to write detailed replies quickly, especially on small on-screen keyboards and in demanding mobile contexts. This motivates new text suggestion features that delegate the writing of replies to the system. For example, Smart Reply (Kannan et al., 2016) and Smart Compose (Chen et al., 2019) suggest short email replies and sentence continuations, respectively. This is especially attractive in mobile contexts, where AI serves to reduce cumbersome typing on a small keyboard (Quinn and Zhai, 2016) or to speed up the reply process (Kannan et al., 2016). More broadly, research in AI-mediated communication (AIMC) highlights relevant conceptual dimensions (Hancock et al., 2020), such as the AI’s magnitude of involvement, optimisation goal, and its autonomy. Operationalised as concrete design decisions, for instance, message-level vs sentence-level suggestions come with different degrees of magnitude and autonomy (Fu et al., 2023), and generally optimise for replying faster.

Having different options matters, including those that retain high user control, because people also wish to stay responsible and maintain their agency (Mieczkowski, 2022). Their replies need to adequately consider the complex nature of their professional and private relationships, which currently is hard to get right with generated text (Fu et al., 2024; Liu et al., 2022; Mieczkowski et al., 2021; Robertson et al., 2021). This creates complex tradeoffs for users in navigating the use of AI in their mobile response workflows.

Current mobile email apps offer two approaches to involving AI: (1) sentence completions, which help with manual writing; and (2) reply generations, which seek to entirely replace the need for manual writing. Both come with tradeoffs:

Sentence-level suggestions, often sporadic and utility-gated to avoid showing unsuitable text (Chen et al., 2019), still demand considerable manual typing and do not account for situations in which users would prefer to automate the reply process further, for example, when only a short reply is needed (cf. (Kannan et al., 2016)).

In contrast, message-level support can take important decisions away from the user, pushing them into an editing role if they prefer to respond in a different way compared to what the AI has drafted. Moreover, there are several negative impacts associated with workflows in which the AI generates the first draft, including reducing self-expression and diversity (Li et al., 2024).

Today’s mobile email apps largely follow the same UI and interaction design for AI integration – such as Gmail with Gemini, Outlook with Copilot, and Superhuman and Shortwave: When replying to an email, users decide as a first step if they want to switch to full reply generation. This brings up a pop-up on top of an empty draft view. Here, users can enter a prompt and check the generated reply, before accepting it with a button to be inserted into the draft. Figure 2 shows this.

This design and workflow hides the incoming email while the user is prompting and assessing the quality of the generated reply. At the same time, the draft view is nothing but an empty background to the pop-up. In summary, there is at least one design contradiction in the current industry standard: UI space is wasted while useful information is omitted.

We address the challenge of AI integration into mobile email replies by taking a step back to reconsider the reply workflow more holistically. This leads to our three guiding research questions: To address these questions, we developed a prototype web app (Figure 1), informed by a formative study (N=17). Our design not only bridges the gaps between manual writing, sentence suggestions, and message-level AI. It also supports a new reply workflow that allows users to still see the incoming email while they work on their response, despite the limited screen space of mobile devices.
(1) How might we redesign the mobile email reply UI for flexible and optional AI involvement?
(2) How do users perceive and interact with this design to reply to emails?
(3) What are the specific advantages and drawbacks of this design, and how do they differ from existing approaches?

Concretely, inspired by concepts from mobile microtasking (August et al., 2020; Iqbal et al., 2018), we integrate a sentence-based response interface directly into the incoming email. We refer to this as Content-Driven Local Response (CDLR) since it allows for responding locally within the content – both for the user and the AI. Users tap on sentences they wish to address, which brings up a local response widget. They then reply manually or use sentence suggestions that adapt to their input. Additionally, users can continue their reply process on the level of the whole message, with an AI improvement pass or by manually editing their reply as a whole. In this way, each AI feature remains optional, empowering users to dynamically build their own workflows, ranging from manual writing to fully AI-generated responses.

We evaluated our concept in a user study (N=126) with a functional prototype and two comparative designs (manual writing and message generation as in today’s email apps). We logged interactions and assessed perception with surveys and in-app feedback.

We found that our system takes a new distinct spot in the design space between sentence-level and message-level support, reflected in participants’ subjective feedback and significant differences in interaction metrics. People used it in varying workflows with different degrees of AI involvement, while retaining the benefits of reduced typing and errors.

In summary, we contribute: (1) A novel concept for local sentence-based replies with optional AI support for mobile email communication, (2) its implementation in a functional prototype, and (3) insights from an evaluation with users.

In a broader perspective, our work demonstrates the potential of a new approach to integrating AI capabilities; not by adding them “on top” but rather by focusing on improving the UI for users’ underlying workflows – with and without AI. In this way, designers can empower users to dynamically adjust the desired degree of AI involvement.
We relate our concept and design decisions to research on emailing with AI and the impact of text generation.

subsections.title: 2.1. Text Suggestions for Emailing
paragraphs: There are two main approaches for text suggestions for (mobile) emailing, exemplified by Google’s Smart Compose (Chen et al., 2019) and Smart Reply (Kannan et al., 2016). Smart Compose supports manual writing with sentence completions, while Smart Reply generates short replies to replace manual writing. In contrast, we explore the design space in between by mixing sentence-based and message-based AI involvement.

The study by Fu et al. (2023) compared sentence-level and message-level text suggestions. They found that suggesting whole emails put users in the role of editors, leading to faster task completion. Sentence suggestions, on the other hand, allowed people to retain a greater sense of agency and write more original content. Their call for further design exploration motivates our work: We offer sentence suggestions in one step, followed by an email-level improvement pass – all optional. As Fu et al. (2023) pointed out, designing appropriate AI support for a particular use case requires identifying the “sweet spot” for “suggestion units”. Based on our results, our design provides a new and distinct intermediate option beyond the two existing “extremes” (of pure sentence vs. message-based support).

subsections.title: 2.2. AI Integration in Current Email Apps
paragraphs: Recently, many mobile email apps have added generative AI features, including Google’s Gmail with Gemini, Microsoft’s Outlook with Copilot, and the apps by Superhuman111https://superhuman.com/ and Shortwave.222https://www.shortwave.com/ai-email/a/ As shown in Figure 2, they share a common UI and interaction design: When replying to an email, users can open a pop-up to enter a prompt and trigger generation. The result is shown in the pop-up and can be accepted with a button. It is then inserted into the draft view for sending or editing.

This design hides the email while working with AI to respond to it. Hence, the user needs to remember all relevant information in the email while prompting and checking generations. At the same time, the draft view is an empty backdrop to the AI pop-up. In short: UI space is wasted while useful information is omitted.

Thus, we diagnose that AI is not optimally integrated for mobile email replies: It is added (literally) “on top” of the existing UI. This motivates our approach to take a step back to redesign the UI with users’ workflows in mind – also those without AI.

subsections.title: 2.3. Learning from Mobile Microtasking
paragraphs: Microtasking principles provide inspiration for our redesign: Limited screen space (Raptis et al., 2013) and frequent interruptions (Leiva et al., 2012; Oulasvirta et al., 2005), along with input method constraints (Palin et al., 2019), make it difficult to work on text documents on mobile touchscreen devices. This motivates research on mobile microtasking (Cheng et al., 2015) to break down larger tasks, such as writing a document, into manageable chunks that can be completed in short, opportune moments on the go, one at a time (August et al., 2020; Iqbal et al., 2018).

The success of microtasking for mobile writing has not yet been brought to email. Possibly, emailing itself is seen as a microtask, as checking mails is fast (cf. (Bao et al., 2011; Oulasvirta et al., 2012; Zheng and Gao, 2024)) and a common productive task while waiting, as shown by Zheng and Gao (2024). However, productive tasks were less likely with only a phone, highlighting the gap between checking and responding. It is also likely that short emails do not require microtasking. That said, emailing “on the go” may involve interruptions (Oulasvirta et al., 2005), which motivate keeping relevant context information in view, even for shorter emails.

Together, this motivates our redesign of the response workflow with microtasking in mind: Traditionally, users read an email and then type into a separate, empty text box. Instead, we allow users to write short responses in boxes shown directly within the incoming email text.

This acknowledges that writing depends on local context (Salehi et al., 2017), which should be shown in microtasking todos (August et al., 2020; Iqbal et al., 2018). Unlike the related work, we do not assume that users go to a desktop computer to finalise their text. Thus, we add a second UI view where users can finalise their reply, for example by connecting partial responses coherently. Our design offers optional AI support for this.

subsections.title: 2.4. Design Factors for Mobile Emailing with AI
paragraphs: Park et al. (2019) revealed user needs for inboxes that also motivate our design for responding to a single email: Support for attention management, a presentation closer to the lighter interaction of messaging UIs, and breaking up longer emails. In line with this, our concept allows users to respond to longer emails in more lightweight chunks by writing local responses to parts of an email. Related, Rambler by Lin et al. (2024) provides text cells to break up text while drafting and editing with text-to-speech input and AI features on tablets.

We further use insights from related work on the number and type of suggestions (Buschek et al., 2021; Dang et al., 2023): Our design offers multiple suggestions that users can refine. Unlike the related work (Dang et al., 2023), users can use the same UI element (text box) to either type their own response or enter a prompt to refine the suggestions. This supports dynamic user decisions about AI involvement without the need for separate UI elements for writing and prompting, taking into account the limited space of mobile screens.

To support emailing for people with dyslexia, LaMPost (Goodman et al., 2022) offers AI rewriting and subject line generation. Similar to Wordcraft (Yuan et al., 2022), which is not email-specific, the design allows users to select text with the mouse to then trigger AI support. We bring this pattern of “selection for local AI support” to mobile touchscreens, with two adaptations: (1) selecting whole sentences rather than character-level selection, to account for the limited accuracy of touch; and (2) displaying suggestions directly below the selection, rather than in a sidebar, which would not fit on a mobile screen.

subsections.title: 2.5. Impact and Perception of Generated Text
paragraphs: Work in AI-mediated communication (AIMC (Hancock et al., 2020)) reveals the complexity of social issues involved in writing emails with AI-generated text: Robertson et al. (2021) studied how people edit suggested email texts and found that suggestions can be inappropriate, with respect to intersecting factors, including relationship types and cultural norms. Related, Lucy et al. (2024) found that the desired appropriate system behaviour in this context is highly individual.

Focusing on the receivers instead, Liu et al. (2022) studied perception and trust towards emails, including the perceived degrees of AI involvement and interpersonal emphasis. Overall, trust in writers decreased when told about AI involvement, yet the findings also suggested differences between people’s statements about AI and actual reactions, indicating an ongoing development of perspectives.

Together, the above results show that it is difficult to provide text generations for emails that entirely avoid problematic suggestions. Thus, Robertson et al. (2021) called for more studies on designs that explore “Giving users more control” beyond one-shot generation, based on their finding that “a large fraction of [suggested] replies were amended”. This directly motivates our design, which allows users to control – on a sentence-level – in the incoming email (1) what to respond to at all, (2) whether to involve AI here, and (3) if so, what additional information to give the AI for that involvement (e.g. by entering keywords to refine the suggestions).

Related, Li et al. (2024) studied people’s choices of AI assistance in controlled argumentative and creative writing tasks at the desktop. They report on desirable and undesirable changes on several metrics, depending on whether the “primary” writer is the human or the AI. All identified negative impacts arise in the “AI-primary” case, where ChatGPT provides a first draft. Thus, their findings motivate our exploration beyond such “AI-primary” designs for emails. Our results extend the bigger picture to mobile text entry and emailing; for example, in line with their results, our proposed design retains more content diversity than the “AI-primary” baseline.

We introduce the concept of content-driven local response. At a high level, we take inspiration from mobile microtasking UIs and integrate a local response interface directly into the incoming email. Overall, our final design is situated in the unexplored space in between sentence-level and message-level approaches for AI support.

subsections.title: 3.1. Design Goals and Rationale
paragraphs: With insights from the literature (Section 2), we designed our system with several goals in mind. For each goal, we briefly mention our approach here, with more details on its final realisation in Section 4.

(1) Human decides, AI supports: The user should be able to make all important decisions, while AI supports these. In our design, the user selects the sentences they want to reply to. The system then suggests response sentences, designed to offer a mix of positive, neutral, and negative responses.

(2) The user stays in control: The user should stay in control of their reply. The AI should not make unnoticed or unwanted adjustments. Our system does not change text unless requested and the user can always edit the reply before sending it.

(3) Support mobile microtasking: The user should be able to leverage microtasking principles for mobile email replies. Our design provides the surrounding email as context while entering reply text and thus shifts from recall to recognition by eliminating the need to remember the email or scroll back to it.

(4) Support diverse workflows – with and without AI: In each situation, users should be able to answer in their preferred way. Our skippable components offer flexibility. Even without AI, users get supportive microtasking structure. Conversely, users can choose to rely on AI text to respond fast, with little typing.

subsections.title: 3.2. First Prototype
paragraphs: We implemented the concept as an Android app with React Native.333https://reactnative.dev/ At this point, the prototype had our sentence-based mode as shown in Figure 3, with three screens. The first screen (Figure 3 left) showed the email for users to select sentences via touch. Each selection triggered a card view that displayed AI suggestions and a text box for entering text (as a manual response or as a prompt to refine the suggestions). The LLM always generated two positive, two negative, and two neutral answering options. One positive and one negative suggestion were shown on the first page, if possible. Users could click on the arrows on the sides to access the others. A second screen (Figure 3 centre) supported manual editing of paragraphs, inserting new ones and/or reordering them via drag and drop. Finally, a third screen showed the result in a standard text editor view for a last check and final edits, if necessary (Figure 3 right).

subsections.title: 3.3. Formative Study
paragraphs: We conducted a first study to understand how users perceive and interact with our concept, and to inform a design iteration. We recruited 17 participants (1 female, 15 male, 1 preferred not to disclose) from our university network. The study followed our institute’s regulations, including information on goals, process, data recording, opt-out and consent.

Participants used our prototype on their own phones. They received a tutorial beforehand. The app did not integrate with actual email accounts to preserve privacy. Instead, it simulated to receive two emails per day, for five days. These emails were quite long, ranging from 140 to 491 words per email (median: 227), to allow participants to test the prototype extensively. People were asked to respond to the emails in a reasonable time frame.

After each reply, the app displayed three 5-point Likert items (“The AI tool was helpful”, “The AI tool helped me reply to the email faster”, “The AI tool helped me write a better reply”) and space for open feedback. Following the final email, participants completed a questionnaire about their overall experience and demographics.

subsections.title: 3.4. Results
paragraphs: The median time of engaging with each email task in our study was 6.9 minutes, including the time taken to enter feedback. People accepted 9.17 suggestions per email. Nearly 80 %80\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} of accepted suggestions were accepted without making use of the text input for refinement. In 30 %30\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} of emails, participants composed the email entirely with suggestions without edits afterwards. When they indeed made edits, the most common ones we identified through manual coding were the following: On the first screen, they removed text (25 instances), added information (11), changed details (8), shortened text (6), and added salutations (5) or closing statements (5). On the second screen, they reordered or merged sentences and paragraphs (35), shortened text (10), and changed minor details (8). They made similar edits on the third screen.

The Likert results (Figure 10 in Section A.3) indicated that participants found the AI to be helpful and that it supported them in writing the replies. They felt in control of the email content and found the suggestions to make sense and not be distracting. They generally agreed that the approach helped them remember to address all parts of an email. However, they were more divided on whether they overall preferred the step-by-step process or the traditional one-step approach for replying to emails.

Open in-app feedback was provided by 14 out of 17 participants. Positive aspects mentioned there and in the final questionnaire included ease of use, faster replies, the quality and inspirational potential of AI suggestions, and an overall improved workflow.

Negative aspects included slow AI response times, quality of suggestions (e.g. too short or not aligning with their input), minor bugs (e.g. failure to load suggestions), and the number of steps (e.g. some suggested to merge the last two screens into one).

The final questionnaire asked people to reflect on their workflow with our app. They reported different strategies, such as generating custom replies with keywords, reading the entire email before replying, or reviewing generated suggestions first. Some manually merged or adjusted AI-generated text, while others used it as is. The final questionnaire also included the System Usability Scale (SUS) (Brooke et al., 1996). The mean score was 78.67 (“very good”, details in Figure 11 in Section A.3).

subsections.title: 3.5. Prototype Iteration
paragraphs: In summary, the findings from this first study indicated that participants appreciated our concept as it helped them to write fast and high-quality replies with AI, while still feeling in control. It also revealed individual approaches when answering emails and interacting with the suggestions. Based on the study insights, we made the following concrete changes to our prototype:

• Reduced number of steps: We removed the second screen (Figure 3 centre) and direly offered the third one for free text editing and finalising. Some participants suggested this and they overall made very similar edits across these two screens.

• Added optional improvement pass: We added an “improve email” button to the final screen to better support users’ varying strategies and preferences for answering emails with AI. We observed that some participants manually edited their emails to create transitions between individual paragraphs generated on the first screen. The improvement pass feature automates this process, adding missing greetings, sign-offs, and correcting grammar and spelling (see Section A.1.3 for the used prompt).

• Faster suggestion generation: We switched from GPT-3.5 Turbo444https://platform.openai.com/docs/models to Llama 3 8B Instruct555https://huggingface.co/meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct(AI@Meta, 2024), which we hosted locally to reduce latency and avoid request failures. While we did not conduct an in-depth evaluation of the models, we compared a set of generations qualitatively and found that the output quality was similar for our use case. Related, we envision that real-world applications could rely on smaller models that can be executed on devices locally to avoid the need to send private email content to a model provider.

• Refined suggestions: We refined our prompting templates to improve suggestions, even with the smaller model. Our new prompts included more context, i.e. all sentence-level replies that were already given up to this point.

• Port to a React web app: We ported the app from React Native to a React web app and optimised it. This eased access for participants, as running a React Native prototype required several steps for setup. Instead, a React web app can be accessed via a web browser on any smartphone.

In the rest of the paper, we always refer to the improved prototype. We next describe it in detail.
We implemented a frontend and backend, which preprocessed emails, logged user data, and generated responses.

subsections.title: 4.1. Frontend
paragraphs: We implemented our web app with the React666https://react.dev/ framework.

This view matches standard mobile email UIs: It includes the sender’s name and picture, the email subject, and the main text body (Figure 1 left). The user can select sentences in the incoming email by tapping on them (cf. design goal 1: Human decides, AI supports; and goal 3: Support mobile microtasking). This opens the local response widget (Section 4.1.2). The “Finalize Reply” button at the bottom of the UI switches to the next screen (Figure 1 centre), which we describe in Section 4.1.3. In accordance with design goal 4 (Support diverse workflows – with and without AI), no interaction with any sentence or AI feature is required before proceeding to this next screen.

This UI widget is inserted into the email text below the user’s selected sentence. It comprises of a text field (Figure 1 C) and a paginated card view that shows text suggestions (Figure 1 D). In the text field, users can enter both manual responses or prompts to refine these suggestions (Figure 4).

Concretely, the widget offers six suggestions (2 positive, 2 negative, 2 neutral), showing two at once. The system aims to show one positive and one negative response on the first page, if possible. This was motivated by findings on positivity bias in AI-generated communication text (Mieczkowski et al., 2021) and to increase the chance of offering a response option fitting to the user’s intent (cf. (Kannan et al., 2016)). We realised this by prompting the LLM to do so (see Section A.1.1). Concretely, the variable “attribute” in the prompt template was replaced with accepting, declining, and neutral to generate varying suggestions. In our tests, we observed that this simple prompting approach worked well and that it did not negatively impact generated suggestions in cases where these attributes do not apply (e.g. our “cat” example in Figure 1D). Users can navigate through suggestions using the adjacent arrow buttons. They can accept a suggestion by tapping on it.

The widget has two states – open and collapsed (Figure 1 A, B): It is collapsed by tapping the currently selected sentence again, by selecting a different sentence, by accepting a suggestion, or by clicking on the check mark in the top right corner. When the text field is empty, the check mark transforms into a trash icon to delete the local reply. Multiple widgets can be in the collapsed state throughout the email but only one widget at a time can be open and in focus. A widget’s text is shown in the collapsed state. This allows users to keep track of all their local replies so far. Tapping on a collapsed widget opens it again for further editing.

This view (Figure 1 centre) shows the current state of the reply after the local response step. That is, it displays any text entered in response to individually selected sentences together in a single text field.

Users can manually adjust this text and/or tap the “Improve” button to request the AI to enhance the email. This improvement pass feature is realised with a prompt (see Section A.1.3) to the underlying LLM to correct spelling and grammar, refine wording, and add missing salutations or regards while adhering to both the incoming email’s content and the existing reply text.

If no text is entered first, the “Improve” button acts as message-level support, generating a reply based on the incoming email’s text and the current input on this screen. For example, a user could skip the local response and enter a prompt here, effectively realising a message generation workflow similar to the industry default (Section 2.2). This flexibility contributes to our design goal 4 (Support diverse workflows – with and without AI).
When the user is satisfied with their reply, the email can be sent by tapping the “Send Email” button at the bottom of this screen.

The improvement pass feature does not change the user’s text directly, in line with our design goal 2 (The user stays in control). Instead, the new text is shown in a pop-up view with formatting familiar from “track changes” in text editors (Figure 1 right). Users can approve these changes, which updates the text, or discard them (cf. design goal 1: Human decides, AI supports.) Further editing after acceptance and/or requesting improvements repeatedly is possible.

subsections.title: 4.2. Backend
paragraphs: Our prototype’s backend has three purposes: (1) It hosts the web app on a Next.js server. (2) It provides email preprocessing, which handles tasks such as sentence-splitting and making API calls to the LLM. (3) It hosts the LLM.

We experimented with several models and APIs and discussed factors such as latency, stability of service, and subjective response quality in meetings with all authors. Based on this, we used the Llama 3 8B Instruct (AI@Meta, 2024) model for the main study.

Similarly, we iterated over several prompting approaches for the text generation features. Overall, this resulted in a few-shot approach, providing the model with several input-output examples to generate fitting responses. As an overview, we use the following prompt templates (details in Section A.1):

We prompted six suggestions for the sentence selected in the email (2 positive, 2 neutral, 2 negative). This balanced the options, following related work (Kannan et al., 2016), as the LLM favoured positive responses in our tests.

This was identical to the above case but now it included the user’s input in addition to the selected sentence. We emphasised alignment with the user’s sentiment (e.g. no negative suggestions if the user had entered “yes”).

We prompted the LLM to answer to the whole email, also by taking into account any current user input, if available. A variation of this was also used for the improvement pass feature (Section 4.1.4). That prompt emphasised improving the current state of the reply while closely adhering to the information provided by the user.

For the main study, we switched from the field study design of the formative study to a more controlled web-based experiment. Our motivation was to scale the study, to compare interaction designs quantitatively, and to introduce task briefings that would allow us to evaluate if people accept even unfitting suggestions for convenience in the study. Thus, we conducted a within-subject user study with our iterated prototype. The independent variable UImode had three levels: Content-driven local response (CDLR) – our proposed design (Section 4); message-level reply generation (MSG) – a one-prompt reply generation design close to currently available UIs (Section 2.2); and writing without any AI features (NoAI). As dependent variables, we logged interaction metrics and collected subjective feedback via questionnaires.

subsections.title: 5.1. Apparatus
paragraphs: For CDLR, we hosted our prototype (Section 4) as a web app, with added study information, study logic, and logging. We integrated a screen for briefings (Section 5.1.3) before each email task and one with four Likert items (Section 5.3.2) after each email task (Figure 12 in Section A.3). We used a custom study framework to manage counterbalancing and the flow from consent information to prototypes to surveys.

We implemented two alternatives for the study: For NoAI, the app only showed a typical drafting view (Figure 13 in Section A.3). For MSG, it was designed similar to the typical UI pattern shown in Figure 2 – it offered a text field for (optional) prompting and displayed the generated text (Figure 14 in Section A.3). Accepting the text with a button inserted it into a draft view for further editing or sending. Rejecting it allowed users to refine their prompt and generate a new draft.

We prepared nine emails, covering an idea pitch contest, a high school reunion, a sales offer, a lunch meeting, a marketing slogan, proofreading for a friend, a sales report deadline, server access, and a gift idea for a retiring coworker. This set was motivated to cover various plausible email topics, with and without (multiple) questions. It also covered various emails lengths, ranging from 24 to 155 words (median 57).

We also prepared a reply briefing for each email. It provided information relevant for what to answer, without specifying how to write (e.g. tone, structure). For example, for the high school reunion email, the briefing specified that the user was unavailable on a certain date. These briefings were not given to the LLM, which would have simulated unrealistic “mindreading”. In contrast, our motivation for the briefings was to assess to what extent participants might accept unfitting suggestions for convenience. Moreover, the briefings mimic an email workflow where some information is readily available while details may need to be retrieved. For instance, in the high school example, reading the briefing could be seen as similar to checking a calendar app.

subsections.title: 5.2. Participants
paragraphs: We recruited 162 participants through the online platform Prolific.777https://www.prolific.com/ We excluded 36 participants from our analysis because they either did not complete all tasks (18 participants) or the logs indicated that a technical issue had occurred (18 participants). Our analyses are based on the remaining 126 participants (83 male, 40 female, 1 non-binary, 2 preferred not to disclose). Their age ranged from 18 to 72 years (median 32). All were proficient in English (91.3% native speakers). Their occupations included both professions where frequent email usage is expected (e.g. IT consultant, project manager) and others (e.g. gardener, waiter). Participants were compensated with about £10 per hour.

Most participants reported to answer emails at least once a day (48.41 %48.41\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}) or even more than 10 times a day (21.43 %21.43\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}). Another 16.67 %16.67\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} answer emails at least once a week, 7.94 %7.94\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} less than once a week, and 5.56 %5.56\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} less than once a month.

Most participants (85.71 %85.71\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}) use their smartphone for answering emails. Many also use a laptop (82.54 %82.54\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}) or desktop computer (53.97 %53.97\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}). Some also use a tablet (19.05 %19.05\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}). They answer emails at home (84.92 %84.92\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}), at the office (69.84 %69.84\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}), and on the go (53.17 %53.17\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}). Many answer emails for business (84.92 %84.92\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}) and in a private context (58.73 %58.73\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}).

Only 14.29 %14.29\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} reported no previous experience with AI. Many have used ChatGPT (72.22 %72.22\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}). Many have experience with auto-correction (51.59 %51.59\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}), some also with auto-completion (26.98 %26.98\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}), with word or sentence suggestions (21.42 %21.42\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}), and with Smart Reply (15.87 %15.87\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}). An overview of all questions and answer options can be found in Section A.2.

subsections.title: 5.3. Procedure
paragraphs: The study was conducted remotely on participants’ own smartphones. Access via Prolific was restricted to one person at a time to balance the load for our server. The sessions were scheduled for 45 minutes and structured as follows:

An introduction page explained the study, including information about GDPR compliance, privacy, data collection, and informed consent, in accordance with our institutional regulations. In addition, whenever encountering a UI for the first time, our study framework showed an explanation of its features.

Participants were asked to reply to nine given emails, three per UImode (CDLR, MSG, NoAI). We counterbalanced the email topics and also the order of the UIs with a Latin square design to address potential learning or fatigue effects.

For each email task, the briefing (Section 5.1.3) was shown at the start and could be accessed again anytime via the information button in the top right corner of the app (Figure 1). Participants were instructed to “consider the information in the briefing for answering the email[s]”.

After submitting each email, participants rated four Likert items: “The app interface was helpful”, “The app interface helped me reply to the email quickly”, “The app interface helped me write a good reply”, and “I was in control of the content of my reply”. They could share comments in a text field.

This questionnaire was displayed after the final email task. Participants provided demographics, selected their favourite UI mode, and explained their choice. They could also leave comments, questions, and feedback.

subsections.title: 5.4. Qualitative Analysis
paragraphs: Here we describe our approach to coding open feedback and analysing email texts.

We followed Grounded Theory (Corbin, 1990) to analyse participants’ open feedback. In the open coding round, two researchers independently reviewed the data, identifying and labelling sentences that represent specific ideas and principles. We then refined these initial codes by merging and clustering related ones, forming (sub-)categories during an axial coding round. Our research team discussed emerging themes, leading to synthesised, overarching labels for the clusters and, in some cases, further split categories to capture more nuanced insights from the feedback. We repeated this process until reaching consensus.

Assessing email quality is complex and subjective, as known from studies on people’s preferences (cf. (Liu et al., 2022; Robertson et al., 2021)). Therefore, we use multiple quality indicators: Formal indicators (Reeves, 2008; Lewin-Jones, 2014) include the presence of a salutation and a closing statement (Section 6.3.5), and proper spelling and grammar (Section 6.3.2). We also analysed briefing conformity, that is, we checked whether replies covered the key information provided in the briefings (Section 6.3.6). Finally, we share our subjective impressions (Section 6.3.7).

We employed Binary Coding (Miles et al., 2013) to assess the briefing conformity as well as the formal indicators, except spelling and grammar, which we checked using the language-tool-python888https://pypi.org/project/language-tool-python/ library. Two researchers coded all email replies independently, assigning a “1” if the email met the criteria and a “0” if it did not. Ambiguous emails were flagged for further review. In a second round, these were re-evaluated by the research team until reaching consensus.

subsections.title: 5.5. Statistical Analysis
paragraphs: To declutter our following report, Table 1 and Table 2 summarise the statistical analyses and results referred to throughout Section 6. We computed (generalised) linear mixed-effects models (LMMs) in R (R Core Team, 2020), using the packages lme4 (Bates et al., 2015) and lmerTest (Kuznetsova et al., 2017). These models accounted for the individual differences between participants and for differences between the incoming emails via random intercepts.

The models’ fixed effects were UImode and whether the improvement pass feature was used in CDLR. For the model for briefing conformity, we additionally included a predictor for whether the reply was generated without user input, such that the result was generated fully by the LLM based on the incoming email only. In MSG, this is done by not entering a prompt for the reply generation. In CDLR, this is done by not providing any local response (manual or suggestion) on screen 1, before using the improvement pass feature on screen 2. Pairwise comparisons were computed with the emmeans package with Bonferroni-Holm correction.
For the Likert data, we used rank-aligned repeated measures ANOVA (ART) (Wobbrock et al., 2011) and ART-C contrasts with Bonferroni-Holm correction for the follow-up analysis (Elkin et al., 2021).
We report significance at p ¡ 0.05.

subsections.title: 6.1. Analysis of Interaction Logs
paragraphs: We report our analyses of the interaction data.

On average, participants took 3.20 min3.20\text{\,}\mathrm{min} (SD 2.51, median 2.46) to write an email manually. With MSG, this decreased to 2.03 min2.03\text{\,}\mathrm{min} (SD 1.90, median 1.44), while CDLR reduced it to 2.95 min2.95\text{\,}\mathrm{min} (SD 2.49, median 2.32). For CDLR, using the improvement pass feature resulted in 2.90 min2.90\text{\,}\mathrm{min} (SD 2.58, median 2.26), while not using it had 3.06 min3.06\text{\,}\mathrm{min} (SD 2.29, median 2.57). Figure 5 (left) shows this as box plots. These differences were significant as follows (Table 1, row 1): Participants finished replying significantly faster with MSG than without AI (-70 s70\text{\,}\mathrm{s}). MSG was also significantly faster than CDLR (-66 s66\text{\,}\mathrm{s}).

On average, participants wrote 2.05 characters per second without AI (SD 1.57, median 1.78). Replying with MSG had a mean of 7.21 (SD 7.90, median 4.89), while the mean speed with CDLR was 4.38 (SD 5.59, median 2.91). For CDLR, using the improvement pass feature resulted in 5.03 (SD 6.47, median 3.23), while not using it had 2.91 (SD 2.00, median 2.31). Figure 5 (right) shows this as box plots. These differences were significant as follows (Table 1, row 2): Compared to writing without AI, participants produced significantly more characters per second with MSG (5.2 chars more per s) and if they used the improvement pass feature in CDLR (2.5 chars more per s). The difference between MSG and CDLR was also significant.

Without AI, participants on average needed 321.69 keystrokes (SD 217.76, median 284), compared to 146.67 (SD 144.97, median 108) with MSG, and 174.62 (SD 166.57, median 128) with CDLR. For CDLR, using the improvement pass feature had a mean of 176.0 (SD 173.7, median 126), while not using it had 171.4 (SD 149.9, median 133.5). Figure 5 (centre) shows this as box plots. These differences were significant (Table 1, row 3): People needed significantly fewer keystrokes with AI features than without them, and even significantly fewer with MSG (58 %58\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} decrease) than with CDLR (48 %48\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} decrease). Using the improvement pass feature in CDLR significantly reduced this further for that UI (5.9 %5.9\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} decrease). In summary, all AI features significantly reduced manual typing.

We logged interactions specific to CDLR. On average participants tapped on 2.64 (SD 2.89, median 2) sentences per email, that is, on 30.36 %30.36\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} (SD 29.59 %29.59\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}, median 23.08 %23.08\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}) of sentences in each email. They replied to 87.37 %87.37\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} of tapped sentences. In 83.27 %83.27\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} of the cases, they did so by accepting a suggestion.

Suggestions were paginated; most suggestions (67.15 %67.15\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}) were accepted on the first page. Another 19.50 %19.50\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} and 13.36 %13.36\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} were accepted on the second and third page, respectively. The majority of accepted suggestions (80.14 %80.14\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}) were generated without an explicit prompt, and most (92.30 %92.30\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}) were not edited afterwards.

On the first screen, 69.05 %69.05\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} of participants accepted a sentence suggestion at least once, and 55.56 %55.56\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} manually entered text for at least one local response. On the second screen, 83.33 %83.33\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} of participants accepted at least one email-level suggestion. Only 1.59 %1.59\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} (two participants) did not use any CDLR-specific features.

On average, 1.67 min1.67\text{\,}\mathrm{min} (57.23 %57.23\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}) were spent on the first screen and 1.25 min1.25\text{\,}\mathrm{min} (42.77 %42.77\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}) on the second (Figure 7). Participants used the improvement pass feature for 287 emails (75.93 %75.93\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}) and accepted an improved email for 274 emails (72.49 %72.49\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}). When the improvement pass feature was used at least once, an improved email was requested on average 1.35 times (SD 0.95, median 1.00) and accepted 1.13 times (SD 0.47, median 1.00) per email. The last accepted improved email was identical to the sent email in 83.94 %83.94\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} of all cases. When participants made changes these had a mean edit distance of 72.73 (SD 97.10, median 37).

With this UI, 71.98 %71.98\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} of the first generated replies were accepted; otherwise, a new generation was requested. Users spent 0.43 min0.43\text{\,}\mathrm{min} (21.20 %21.20\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}) on the incoming email screen, 1.31 min1.31\text{\,}\mathrm{min} (65.09 %65.09\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}) on the generation view and 0.28 min0.28\text{\,}\mathrm{min} (13.71 %13.71\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}) on the editing screen (Figure 7).

For CDLR, we discovered three main workflows by plotting when people switched from the first screen (Figure 1A) to the second (Figure 1B). Figure 6 reveals three clusters: (1) Sometimes people went straight to the second screen and used the improvement pass feature to create a draft. (2) Alternatively, they spent most of their time drafting on the first screen, with light manual editing on the second. (3) Finally, people partially drafted on the first screen and finished it on the second screen, using AI. We fitted a GMM999Gaussian Mixture Model with 3 components using https://scikit-learn.org/ to estimate the number of emails: Cluster 1 had 136, cluster 2 had 54, and cluster 3 had 188 emails.

We also examined the relationship of the incoming email’s length and whether people skipped the local response screen without entering any text. This was significant (Table 2, row 2): Each additional word (i.e. 5 additional characters) in the incoming email is associated with a 2.46 %2.46\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} decreased chance of skipping the local response step in CDLR.

For MSG, we found that in most cases (77.8 %77.8\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} of emails) people sent the generated drafts without manually editing them further. When they indeed edited them (22.2 %22.2\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} of emails), the mean edit distance between generated and edited version was 64.87 (SD 67.48, median 44.50). This corresponds to typing about twelve words (Kristensson and Vertanen, 2014).

We also analysed how people prompted with MSG: In a majority of cases (82.01 %82.01\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} of emails), participants entered a prompt right away. Otherwise, they generated text solely based on the information in the incoming email. In half of those cases (51.47 %51.47\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}), participants did not accept the result and generated another. In comparison, such a regeneration was only needed in 22.9 %22.9\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} of the cases where participants entered a prompt. We observed a learning effect for some: 40 %40\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} of people started entering a prompt if they were not happy with the initial result generated without a prompt.

subsections.title: 6.2. Perception of Interaction
paragraphs: We analysed participants’ perception of the three UIs.

Participants rated four Likert items in the app after each email (Figure 8). We found statistically significant effects of UImode on all four items – speed (FF(2, 996.11)=433.71, p¡.0001, ηp2\eta_{p}^{2}=.46), control (FF(2, 996.11)=20.60, p¡.0001, ηp2\eta_{p}^{2}=.04), quality (FF(2, 996.13)=466.99, p¡.0001, ηp2\eta_{p}^{2}=.48), and helpfulness (FF(2, 996.1)=430.61, p¡.0001, ηp2\eta_{p}^{2}=.46). Concretely, CDLR and MSG were both rated significantly higher than NoAI on speed (CDLR: tt(996)=23.34, p¡.0001; MSG: tt(996)=27.25, p¡.0001), quality (CDLR: tt(996)=25.72, p¡.0001; MSG: tt(996)=27.18, p¡.0001), and helpfulness (CDLR: tt(996)=24.65, p¡.0001; MSG: tt(996)=26.14, p¡.0001). They were both rated significantly lower than NoAI on control (CDLR: tt(996)=-5.88, p¡.0001; MSG: tt(996)=-5.17, p¡.0001). The only significant difference between the UIs with AI was that MSG was rated higher on speed than CDLR (tt(996)=3.91, p=.0001).

We reviewed the in-app feedback optionally provided after each reply. For both AI modes it was overwhelmingly positive, such as: “Im really enjoying this kind of AI help mode.” (P60, MSG), “It made work easy” (P76, MSG), “Very smooth process, good suggestions for each part.” (P47, CDLR), and “This made my reply look way better.” (P81, CDLR).

The negative feedback was less homogeneous. For MSG, around half of these critiques highlighted difficulties in getting the AI to incorporate specific information, such as: “the AI who seemed to resist wanting to offer access to my colleague” (P25); or “had a bit of trouble trying to get the AI to properly acknowledge that $200 was okay […]” (P37). Related, P53 noted that “Control in replying was lacking, It didn’t give me many options to ’add’ ideas of my own.” People found ways to steer the system; P47 said that “I had to adjust the prompt a few times to get the sort of reply that I was looking for, but it did generate a good reply overall and I was satisfied with the end result.”

Notably, issues with including specific information were rarely mentioned for CDLR. Most of the negative feedback instead concerned the tone: “This was too wordy for an informal email.” (P56), and “The ai was helpful but it made the response feel slightly too formal and professional.” (P49)
For the manual mode, people “had no issues, [and] felt able to use the platform freely and there was no technical faults” (P7), and that “It was just like normal email.” (P60).

Only 4 %4\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} (5 people) preferred NoAI, 49.2 %49.2\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} (62 people) favoured MSG, and 43.7 %43.7\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} (55 people) preferred CDLR. The remaining 3.2 %3.2\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} (4 people) did not pick a favourite.

Notably, the high-level code “Efficiency” occurred in 56.45 %56.45\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} of comments for MSG and 32.73 %32.73\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} for CDLR. “Quality” in 25.82 %25.82\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} for MSG and 30.91 %30.91\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} for CDLR. “Control” in 8.07 %8.07\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} for MSG and 29.09 %29.09\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} for CDLR. “Tailoring” zero times for MSG and 5.46 %5.46\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} for CDLR. The remaining comments were assigned the code “Others” (e.g. P105 “just liked the interface” of MSG and P76 favoured CDLR because it supported them in being creative).

Two out of the four people who did not select a favourite stated that they liked both depending on the “context” (P77). For instance, P19 explained that “both have different advantages in different situations. Single prompt allows to produce a full email much faster so is handy when you are short of time but still want to respond. Sentence based provides the user the ability to create a much more tailored email which can cover all bases.”
The 4 %4\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} (5 people) who preferred NoAI said they were “used to it” (P4) or “confident in [their] writing ability” (P78).

People perceived AI features as helpful and preferred having them. They were divided about their favourite and perceived meaningful tradeoffs between the two designs with AI on control vs efficiency: While people felt in control with all UIs (Section 6.2.1), when reflecting on their favourite, they mentioned control aspects relatively more frequently for CDLR than MSG – and vice versa for efficiency.

subsections.title: 6.3. Analysis of Emails
paragraphs: We analysed the content of the emails. Figure 9 shows four box plots as an overview.

On average, emails written without AI were 302.5 characters long (SD 169.7, median 267). MSG resulted in 536.1 (SD 319.0, median 447.5), while CDLR had 483.0 (SD 285.4, median 382). For CDLR, using the improvement pass feature had a mean length of 523.9 (SD 294.1, median 412.0), while not using it had 390.6 (SD 241.5, median 324.5). These differences were significant (Table 1, row 4): People wrote significantly longer replies with the AI features than without them, and significantly more so with MSG (77 %77\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} increase) than with CDLR (27 %27\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} increase). Using the improvement pass feature in CDLR significantly increased this further for that UI (38 %38\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} increase). In summary, all AI features significantly increased text lengths.

We checked grammar and spelling with the language-tool-python101010https://pypi.org/project/language-tool-python/ library. Per email, we recorded the minimum of British English and American English spell checking to avoid penalising spelling differences. Manual writing had the highest mean error rate of .00375 errors per character. Both AI versions were about half of that: MSG had .00176 and CDLR had .00182. Using the improvement pass feature in CDLR contributed to reducing errors (mean .00147 when using it vs .00260 when not). All these differences were significant (Table 1, row 5). In summary, all AI features significantly reduced error rates.

We analysed the semantic similarity between emails, following related work (Padmakumar and He, 2024). We computed the cosine similarity of the vector embeddings of all pairs of emails written with the same mode and for the same briefing, using the Sentence Transformers library (SBERT111111https://sbert.net, specifically https://huggingface.co/sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2) (Reimers and Gurevych, 2019). As expected from the literature, manual emails had the lowest mean pairwise similarity (.582), that is, they were the most diverse. MSG had the highest similarity (.756), followed by CDLR (.726). Using the improvement pass feature in CDLR contributed to the increase (.676 without using it vs .749 with it). All these differences were significant (Table 1, row 6). In summary, all AI features significantly reduced semantic diversity.

We analysed lexical diversity, as in related work (Fu et al., 2023), with the distinct-2 metric, defined as the number of distinct bigrams divided by the total number of words. Our results match the related work: Writing without AI had the highest mean lexical diversity (.950), MSG lowered it to .930, and CDLR had .924. Using the improvement pass feature in CDLR (almost) closes the gap between CDLR and MSG (.915 without it vs .927 with it). All these differences were significant (Table 1, row 7). In summary, lexical diversity was significantly affected by all AI features, in the way we would expect from related work (Fu et al., 2023): Sentence-level generations decreased it more than message-level generations.

Salutations were missing in 8.5 %8.5\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} of manually written replies and in 10.6 %10.6\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} with CDLR. All replies with MSG had salutations. All replies with CDLR and the improvement pass feature had a salutation. Similarly, only one email with MSG lacked a closing statement, compared to 14.3 %14.3\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} for NoAI and 9.0 %9.0\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} for CDLR. Again, when the improvement pass was used in CDLR, all emails ended with a closing signature.

Each email reply task showed a briefing that asked participants to respond with certain information (see Section 5.3.2). This allowed us to analyse if their emails conformed to this or not (see Section 5.4.2). This varied across the UIs: With CDLR, 23 %23\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} of emails missed a key aspect of the study briefing, compared to 18 %18\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} for MSG and 13 %13\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} for NoAI.

The differences between CDLR and the other two UIs were significant (Table 2, row 1). However, people’s prompting behaviour had a larger impact here: Across CDLR and MSG, generating a full reply without any own input (83 emails in the data) missed a key aspect of the briefing in half of the cases (49 %49\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}). We return to this in the discussion (Section 7.5)

All emails were read by (at least) two researchers. While length and structure varied (Section 6.3.1, Section 6.3.5), we did not notice “nonsense” responses. Even those emails which did not conform to the briefings (Section 6.3.6) reflected the general topic and most would have been believable replies. We noticed that sensible replies can vary drastically – from short responses to elaborate, formal emails. The latter mostly coincided with message-level AI generation. We do not consider this an issue of quality since we did not specify a level of formality to follow. Overall, based on our subjective assessment, we thus concluded that reply quality was suitable across all UIs.

subsections.title: 7.1. Redesigning Mobile Email Replies with Content-Driven Local Response
paragraphs: Our first research question asked: How might we redesign the mobile email reply UI for flexible and optional AI involvement? We proposed to build on microtasking UI principles with our concept of content-driven local response. Here, we reflect on two key aspects of this solution strategy.

A key benefit of our CDLR design is that it turns the email content into a UI to locally trigger responses. As our results show, users leveraged this to keep the email in view while responding (Figure 7). One way of looking at this is through the lens of the usability principle of “recognition over recall” (Nielsen, 1994): Users can respond and/or delegate tasks to the AI at the very moment of recognising a line in the email as relevant. In contrast, reading a whole email first, to then transition to a draft view (AI-based or manual) requires recalling the email’s information – or scrolling/swiping back and forth, at least on a mobile screen that does not fit email and draft in parallel. This seems more important for complex emails and less relevant for short ones. Our results support this (local response was less likely to be skipped for longer emails, see Section 6.1.6) and motivate a dedicated future study on microtasking UIs for emails of varying complexity. That said, mobile interaction is prone to attentional demands and interruptions (Oulasvirta et al., 2005), which might make it more likely to forget about a relevant aspect not only for very long emails.

In another lens, CDLR responds to the call by Tankelevitch et al. (2024) for UIs that reduce metacognitive demands of generative AI, for example, when deciding when and what to delegate to AI. Our concept was successful here, as echoed by participants’ comments. For example, P24 said “[I could] organize my ideas better using the sentence replies within the email.” Similarly, P8 found that “This was definitely helpful in breaking down the email and specifically answering the important key points of the original message.”
Put as a design recommendation: Enable people to immediately act in the local context of a recognised need for AI support, when the relevant information for that task delegation is present.

CDLR facilitates local responses by both user and AI. For the user, traditional email UIs offer a two-step workflow – read the email, then write a reply. However, the actual workflow has more steps: Users need to identify relevant information, think about their response, and compose a coherent reply. The sentence-wise nature of CDLR makes these steps explicit in the UI. Work on microtasking shows that splitting up writing tasks and presenting them in context is beneficial for mobile users (August et al., 2020; Iqbal et al., 2018).

In our design this additionally supports prompting the AI (see Section 7.6). We believe that this solution strategy is relevant more broadly: Generative AI is often added “on top” of existing UIs (Section 2.2). In contrast, we propose to identify relevant (micro) decisions and response moments in the task, redesign the UI so that users can express these through interaction (here: select sentence), and then integrate AI specifically at these moments.

subsections.title: 7.2. Empirical Characteristics ofCDLR
paragraphs: Our empirical research questions asked: How do users perceive and interact with CDLR? and What are the specific advantages and drawbacks? Here, we reflect on this in comparison to the other UIs in our study.

Our study revealed how CDLR grants users high flexibility. Concretely, people achieve different workflows by varying their use of local responses in combination with AI support features. We identified three clusters (Figure 6). This demonstrates that people have varying preferences when responding to emails with AI, even in a controlled study setting, which CDLR was able to support. Concretely, the observed workflows differed in when and how people transitioned from the local response view to the “global” draft view (Section 6.1.6).
All AI features across CDLR and MSG shared these significant influences: reduced manual typing and error rates, with increased reply lengths and reduced semantic and lexical diversity.

Despite these shared influences, CDLR showed a lower “AI footprint”. As estimated with our statistical models, with MSG (compared to CDLR) people finished their replies even faster (-66 s66\text{\,}\mathrm{s}), with even fewer manual keystrokes (-20 %20\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}), while producing even longer replies (+28 %28\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}). Producing more, faster, with less input indicates a dearth of human intention in writing, as recently highlighted by Kreminski (2024). Indeed, MSG resulted in the lowest diversity between emails. Together with our subjective inspection of the emails (Section 6.3.7), this paints the picture of somewhat bloated replies with full reply generation (MSG). This is in line with the finding by Fu et al. (2024) that verbosity is a key drawback noticed by users of AI tools for communication. As our study shows, people could keep this under control better with CDLR. In summary, CDLR allows users to benefit from AI in a more nuanced and controlled manner.

Manual control might not always be the user’s main concern. CDLR also accounts for workflows with stronger delegation to AI, as shown by clustering the workflows (Figure 6) and by factoring out the impact of the improvement pass feature. If it was used, it narrowed the differences between CDLR and MSG along several metrics (Table 1), which fits to the literature that compared sentence-level vs message-level AI support (Fu et al., 2023). In conclusion, by making use of the optional improvement pass in CDLR, users can situationally vary the described tradeoffs between MSG and our local response design.

subsections.title: 7.3. Deciding Between Sentence- and Message-level Support (CDLRvsMSG)
paragraphs: Participants’ reasonings for their favourite UI (Section 6.2.3) help us understand which types of tasks might benefit from which type of AI support. When favouring MSG, they referred to aspects of efficiency in the majority of their comments, in line with quantitative results. This indicates that this type of support might be favourable for communication tasks in which users deem the speed of their reply to be more relevant than the exact wording (e.g. decision-focused emails, such as accepting or declining a time-sensitive request).

In contrast, for CDLR, participants emphasised quality and control aspects relatively more frequently. Thus, this type of support lends itself to communication tasks that require more careful and/or personal wording. Concretely, Robertson et al. (2021) identified relevant themes in emailing for this, such as caring about personal authenticity, semantic and tonal coherence, relationship types, and norms and culture. Related, Mieczkowski (2022) identified dimensions of agency in AIMC. Our CDLR design and participants’ comments on it fit to their findings about people’s strategic thinking for maintaining agency (e.g. ability for choosing, editing, replacing, not-sending AI text, and/or seeing its changes due to own input).

Supporting this interpretation, comments by those who did not pick a favourite, such as P19, described that the choice depends on contextual factors, such as using MSG under time pressure, while using CDLR when a “tailored” reply was needed.

Related, we found evidence that the value of local responses is higher for longer emails (Section 6.1.6). Future work could investigate the underlying factors in detail. For example, the value of the local response step (and thus sentence-level support) might depend not only on the length but also on aspects of complexity of the incoming email (e.g. emails with multiple questions or describing a complex “history” of a situation as background for a request).

Finally, while we separated the concepts for our comparative study, a real email app could combine the UIs for CDLR and MSG. Note that our CDLR design already supports a message-level workflow by skipping to screen 2 and using the improvement pass feature. It was sometimes already used in this way (Section 6.1.6), as discussed next.

subsections.title: 7.4. Combined Sentence- and Message-level Support Offers Nuanced Control over AI Involvement
paragraphs: Evaluating our CDLR concept also allowed us to gain insights into the combination of different levels of writing support, not studied so far in the literature. While related work addressed AI on either sentence-level or message-level (Fu et al., 2023; Chen et al., 2019; Kannan et al., 2016), we explored the design space in between, with a flexible mix. We found that this combination gives users more nuanced control over how they involve AI.

Concretely, the way people mixed the different degrees of engaging with AI in CDLR (Section 6.1.6) had a meaningful impact on metrics of interaction and outcome (Section 7.2). Fittingly, people appreciated CDLR particularly for aspects related to control and quality (Section 6.2). In summary, we thus conclude that offering optional AI support on different levels is accepted by users in this context and provides more nuanced control over AI involvement and its impact.

Our design partly contradicts product trends, which generate complete replies and focus on workflows that assume that people want to start by delegating more to the AI (Section 2.2). In fact, participants in our study expressed diverging preferences on this, both in behaviour (Section 6.1.6) and reflection (Section 6.2.3). In this context, our findings highlight a variety in workflows that motivates further exploration of flexible designs. Here, our concept of CDLR provides inspiration for offering different levels of optional AI support, to improve various response workflows or writing workflows in general.

subsections.title: 7.5. Reflections on Methodology
paragraphs: Many suggestions in the formative study were accepted without input (Section 3.4), potentially to satisfice, that is, to finish the study faster even with less than ideal results. We thus added briefings in the main study (Section 5.3.2), which provided topic-specific data about how to reply (e.g. unavailable on day X, not selling below a certain price). We did not give these to the LLM, since we did not want to simulate a “mindreading” system.

Interestingly, the ratio of emails that did not cover the briefings’ key aspects was 5 %5\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} higher for CDLR than for message-level reply generation, and 10 %10\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} higher than for manual writing (Section 6.3.6). We explain this as follows: Typing manually, people might as well enter a reply in line with the briefing. In contrast, they might accept AI text, if available, to be fast without checking too closely.

Our data supports this: We found the lowest briefing conformity (50 %50\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}, Section 6.3.6) among replies written without investing in prompts, and thus fully optimised for speed, in both CDLR and MSG. This lack of entering prompts had a much larger impact on conformity than the UI in general (Table 2, row 1). In this light, the 5 %5\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax} difference between CDLR and MSG might be attributed to the fact that MSG only had full response generation, with no other features for users to consider instead of focusing on the one generated text. Additionally, CDLR automatically loads suggestions once a sentence is selected, which might make satisficing more tempting.

More broadly, in a paid online study such as ours, participants likely feel the incentive to complete the task quickly. While real mobile emailing contexts also include situations with time pressure, our study had no negative consequences of writing a less than ideal email. Therefore, the willingness to accept suggestions might be inflated in the study. That said, in real life, people might also have reasons to vary their investment (e.g. based on the importance of the email), which is supported by the flexibility of CDLR. Overall, the timings and thoughtful responses to our (optional) open questions indicate that participants did not merely “skip” through the study. Supporting this, the majority of emails per UI (¿76 %76\text{\,}\mathrm{\char 37\relax}) indeed included the key information from the briefings (Section 6.3.6), and the subjective quality of the writing was high (even when not briefing conform) across all UIs (Section 6.3.7).

In summary, we found evidence that the presence of AI automation features for a study task reduces task adherence, possibly because people then choose different speed-performance tradeoffs for the study and/or monitor their results and performance less closely. This is consistent with findings in related work that people believe that AI improves their performance, even when it does not (Kloft et al., 2024). A future dedicated study could investigate this in more detail since this is potentially relevant for many human-AI interaction studies.

Finally, such studies could let participants themselves or third parties assess the quality of produced texts to capture self-perception and external perception. Following research interests in AI-mediated communication (cf. (Fu et al., 2024; Hancock et al., 2020)), this would focus not only on the sender’s interaction, as in our case here, but also capture the perception of the receivers.

subsections.title: 7.6. Reflections on Prompting: Co-creative Chain-of-Thought Prompting
paragraphs: We use Chain-of-Thought (CoT) as a lens to reflect on our prompting. CoT instructs an LLM to generate intermediate steps (Wei et al., 2022) (e.g. by adding“Let’s think step by step” (Kojima et al., 2022)). In CDLR, users prompt intermediate generation steps by selecting text in the email, while our improvement pass feature then prompts the LLM to generate a full reply from these texts when used at the end. In this view, the user contributes to the intermediate steps on the way to the final result. Thus, this prompting workflow could be seen as a kind of user-involved CoT, or Co-creative CoT (Co-CoT).

This is reflected in people’s comments on prompting: “[CDLR] was the best to express opinions in a way that isn’t just one single prompt, creating a more real feeling of thoughts behind it that AI tends to lack in single prompt emails.” (P117) We believe that this concept is useful more broadly: Rather than only writing a meta-instruction such as “Let’s think step by step”, the user actively contributes to this step-wise “thinking” along the way.

We have proposed the concept of Content-Driven Local Response (CDLR), which allows users to insert concise responses at selected points in the incoming email as they read it. Selecting text for this purpose doubles as an expression of intent to guide the AI’s text suggestions. These local responses can then be further edited manually or again with AI support. This design for the first time combines sentence-level and message-level AI support, while keeping both optional. The key benefit is flexibility. Involving AI with CDLR shares the general characteristics of AI responses but its modularity allows users to make their own choices.

Participants’ comments highlight the distinct strengths of CDLR and the involved tradeoffs. They overall appreciated all AI features but when asked to pick a favourite, their reasoning for message-level AI highlighted efficiency in the majority of cases, while for CDLR they emphasised quality and control relatively more often.
Future work could explore how people make use of CDLR features in real life emailing, with varying social and contextual factors (Robertson et al., 2021), and in long-term use.

While recent feature releases in widely used email apps have focused on AI that generates complete emails, our findings with CDLR motivate the exploration of more flexible alternatives. Rather than adding AI “on top”, we motivate designing for users’ underlying workflows – both for those with and without AI at the same time. In this way, flexible UIs can empower users to dynamically adjust their desired degree of AI involvement to manage tradeoffs, such as between responding quickly and retaining control over the outcome.

subsections.title: A.1. Prompting
paragraphs: Our final prototype used the prompt templates shown below. For a better overview, we shortened them for this appendix by leaving out the concrete few-shot examples and only indicating their position in the templates. See the project repository (link in Section 8) for the full prompts including these examples.
Contained variables are defined as follows:
• sender: The full name of the sender.
• email_text: Text content of the received email.
• existing_reply: Either ‘This is the reply you have written so far: “existing_text” ’ or an empty string if there is no existing text.
• attribute: “accepting”, or “neutral”, or “declining” (two generations for each attribute)
• referenced_text: The sentence that was selected on.
• input: Prompt that the user gave.
